title: E. coli Heat Shock Transformation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9d6re","text":"This describes a method to transform a plasmid into homemade DH5α cells.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"acsu3","text":"Competent cells and any materials that touch them should be disposed of in the biohazardous waste container.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Thaw competent cells on ice. This takes about 10 minutes. Also thaw the plasmid to be transformed.
Warm SOC media to room temperature. undetermined, SOC media per reaction LB media can also be used. Anecdotally, we haven't seen a difference between LB and SOC.

Label a 1.5mL eppendorf tube for each transformation reaction with the construct name and place it on ice. Label an agar plate with the construct, date, your initials, and plate media type (with antibiotic, if applicable). Plate: pYPQ131A 13 March 2018 AR LB+Tet
Heat a dry block to 42ºC, a shaker-incubator to 37ºC, and a cabinet-style incubator to 37ºC.
Add plasmid to the labeled eppendorf tube on ice. undetermined, plasmid

Add thawed competent cells to the tube with the plasmid and gently swirl with the pipet tip to mix. undetermined, competent cells DO NOT vortex. Competent cells have very specific and fragile cell membranes that can be damaged by vortexing.
Incubate the mixture on ice for 30 minutes. 00:30:00, Ice This time is fungible. Longer is better, but as short as 5 minutes will also work.
Place the mixture in the dry block at 42ºC for 30 seconds 00:00:30, Dry Block
Incubate the mixture on ice for 2 minutes. 00:02:00, Ice
Add SOC to the mixture and shake in an incubator 01:00:00, Shake at 37º & 250 rpm undetermined, SOC per reaction
(Optional) Spin the bacterial broth for 30 seconds at 6000 rpm, removed 180µL of supernatant, and gently resuspend the pellet in the remaining media to concentrate. 00:00:30, spin at 6000 rpm

Pipet at least 100µL of the broth onto the labeled agar plate and spread it evenly across the surface of the agar with the sterile spreader. Restilerize the spreader. A larger volume can be used, or you can make two plates with different volumes (e.g. 100 and 50µL) to prevent getting colonies that are too close together.
Immerse a plate spreader in 70% ethanol to sterilize and then burn off the excess alcohol.
Place the prepared plate (lid-down) into a 37ºC incubator overnight (16 hours) The plates can also be incubated at room temperature for several days (the weekend).
creator: Alex Rajewski
